AI Employees are already managing humans.

Last week our AI Employee assigned calls to seven human reps. It was NOT a demo, it was on live traffic.

It read intent, checked capacity, pushed tasks, and flagged escalations, without any ego or hallway politics. Two shifts later the human lead pinged me asking for the AI’s dashboard so he could plan coverage. That was the moment it clicked. Not only we were augmenting or replacing agents, we were re‑wiring even their management.

The strategy is simple. 
- We define the goal for the day. Reduce average handle time, increase right‑party connects or chase a collections milestone. 
- The AI takes the playbook and routes work to the right human at the right moment. 
- It adaptively schedules callbacks, senses sentiment, and escalates only when a human’s judgment can move the needle.

We even saw a quiet shift in accountability. Instead of managers chasing metrics at the end of the week, they now look at our dashboard on real time. The bottlenecks don't hide anymore, coaching has gotten precise, and the best calls get wired in to improve overall output.

I get asked if this threatens people. It does, if they still want to operate in the legacy way.

The team that wins next year is the team that treats AI not just as an employee or a tool enabling them, but also as the floor manager.

The best talent does not follow a single S-curve, as it eventually saturates. They go through multiple S-curves, each one compounding on the last.

When we pivoted from Zywa to CozmoX AI (YC W22), everything changed, except one thing: the need to be tenacious, and adaptive.

The problem we set out to solve was completely different. It demanded new skills, deeper domain expertise, and a whole new mindset. We weren’t just entering a new market, we were stepping into a new industry, with a new playbook (and often, writing it ourselves).

But instead of rebuilding the team, we doubled down on our people.
- ⁠Our iOS engineer became a backend engineer.
- ⁠Our Android engineer reinvented himself as an AI engineer.
- ⁠Our backend engineer set his sights on becoming an ML architect.

Even Nuha and I went through our own crash course, learning the ropes of AI, VAD architecture, Ci/CD pipelines, B2B sales, and how to navigate enterprise decision cycles.

Startups often emphasize deep, vertical expertise, and that’s important. But in our experience, there’s nothing more powerful than gritty generalists, people who are willing to unlearn and relearn everything they know, again and again.

You can't pay your way into PMF or enterprise adoption.

I've built a consumer-facing business before, and now I'm building an enterprise-facing one. With end consumers, you can run campaigns, offer promos, burn money, and change behavior over time. Think Uber, DoorDash.

But enterprises are not impulse buyers, and I’ve learned that you can’t buy your way into their workflows. There’s no way to manufacture urgency if you're not solving real pain, and there's no growth hack to become 'mission critical' for enterprises.

I’ve seen polished demos go nowhere, even when the board was pushing an AI mandate. If the product didn’t solve something painful, it got a polite “no thanks.”

And I’ve seen the opposite too, budgets unlocked overnight when a product hit a real nerve.

Ironically, the only "shortcut" I’ve found to PMF in enterprise is doing the slow, unscalable work of sitting with early adopters, figuring out what’s broken, and co-creating with them.

We grew CozmoX AI (YC W22) to over $1M ARR in 12 weeks by ignoring the generic pattern-matching advice we kept hearing.

So, we were told to 'build anywhere, but sell to the US.'

Sure, that's an advice that has worked for a lot of AI-startups but we knew one truth, the Middle East adopts technology once the value is clear and the trust is local. 

Both Nuha and I grew up in Saudi, and today there's an urgency in the companies in entire GCC and MENA to adopt AI, the leadership is anxious, they have made budgets and AI committees, and the rate of adoption is faster than other technology shifts like cloud or SaaS.

So, we went all-in on MENA. We met decision makers face-to-face, became their consultants, showed the value of our AI Employees and even deployed our team to implement.

GTM decisions are deeply contextual. What worked for others is just a data point, not a rule.

Pattern-matching might feel safe. But it’s context-matching that moves the needle. That’s where the real dollars (okay, dirhams) are.

We fired 4 customers last week, 5% of our revenue. It's one of the hardest things a founder has to do. But here's why it's essential:

In the early days, you say yes to anyone willing to pay. Every dollar helps you survive, and every logo feels like progress. But beyond a point, that survival instinct becomes a growth bottleneck.

Last week, we reviewed usage across a few of our earliest accounts. These were teams that had gone live, but with almost no call volumes, no urgency to scale, no dedicated reps, and no real push for outcomes.

So, we made a framework and decided to filter them out:
- <$20K/month spend = dropped
- No scale = dropped
- Outside our vertical thesis = dropped
- Not going ahead with deeper integrations and workflows = dropped

Honestly, if we hadn’t done it, they would’ve churned anyway. We just went first and ended the relationship with clarity and respect.

At our stage, we don't need passive customers, we need design partners, growth catalysts, and teams that scale with us.

Founders hate doing this because of the sunk cost fallacy, months of effort already invested in the relationship. But the reality is, such relationships are just coexistence without mutual growth. And that’s a drag on both sides.

Retention doesn’t mean keeping everyone. It means choosing who you want to grow with. And not all churn is bad, some of it is just you clearing space for the customers who are actually ready to win.

Founders set a very high bar for what would be 'acceptable' to their customers, and that obsession kills more startups than bad ideas, lack of funding, or competition.

Because it delays reality.

We dodged that 'perfection trap' by starting to sell before fully building our AI Employees. That gave us the right signals to define what a minimum lovable product actually looked like for our customers.

While we wanted to chase near-perfect latency in our AI Employees, our customers didn’t really care about that level of perfection. They cared about the outcomes our AI Employees delivered.

What our first customer signed up for was very different from what we have today. But that early customer has stayed with us, and they’ve loved seeing the product get better every week. That visible progress built more trust than any “perfect” product ever could.

The market doesn’t reward perfection. It rewards momentum.

Your obsession with perfection is just a disguised fear of rejection. Let the market humble you (or celebrate you) quickly while you iterate your product toward PMF.

We just sunset our entire Account Management team, not because customer success matters less, but because the old playbook can't keep pace with the speed of AI-led transformation.

Traditional account management was built for planned delivery, following a scope, managing hand-offs, quarterly check-ins, annual renewals. That works when one-size-fits-all SaaS is acceptable.

But that era is over. Today, enterprises want AI that understands their data, their workflows, their stakes & their environment. And the only way to deliver that is to move away from planned delivery to embedded execution. 

So, we replaced AMs with FDTs, Forward Deployed Teams. 

They are hybrid operators, engineers who sit inside customer environments, observe real workflows, identify friction, build or integrate solutions and co-create deployments that guarantee outcomes. Basically, they pair contextual empathy of customers with our engineering abilities. 

FDT is now the heart of customer success at CozmoX AI (YC W22). I’m betting more AI startups will follow, because the renewal driver has shifted. It's not licences sold anymore, it's the outcomes delivered.

In the past two weeks, two customers and one VC wrote to us saying they love our stance. Because it's honest & because it reflects the real promise of AI. 

Here's our stance: We're here to replace humans, and we're unapologetic about it. 

I've seen founders get flustered when asked if their product would lead to job losses, and then start beating around the bush, saying things like:

- We are unlocking human potential
- We are augmenting humans, not replacing them
- We are giving humans more time to do higher-value work

Let's stop pretending. If you're building something truly valuable, you're replacing someone’s job.

If you’ve built an AI Sales Employee, you're replacing SDRs or contact center agents. The same applies to building an AI Support and Collections Employee.

And if, as a founder, you don't strongly support what you're doing, it's hard to find true believers in your mission. That’s why we say it clearly, and we're not sorry about it.

I believe a founder's role goes beyond selling a product. They are selling a worldview.

Ours is simple: replace slow, expensive labor with fast, scalable intelligence.

Stepping away is one of the hardest things to do, especially when it’s unexpected.

I’ve been in and out of the hospital for the past few days, and while I focus on recovery, my team has been quietly holding the fort. They’ve taken full ownership and kept things moving with super high accountability.

It’s a strange kind of relief to be on the sidelines and still see the engine running without you.

What started as a dream for Nuha and me has truly become a shared, living reality.

Grateful beyond words to Wesley, Nitin, Sahaj, Akash, Amal, Elliott, Sanket, Aarsh, Jayanth, Pawanraj, Pankaj, and Sayan. 

Can’t wait to be back with all of you at the office.

Liza, our AI Sales Employee just earned back her annual salary, in just 5 days.

We recently onboarded a real estate company in NYC that took AI employee training to an entirely new level. After bringing Liza onto their team, they trained her using Jordan Belfort’s Straight Line sales method. They fed her the full suite of his strategies, their internal scripts, objection handling techniques, and key company context.

And once Liza started calling their customers, she called over 10,000 people, she negotiated deals & she booked sales-qualified meetings into our client's calendar. In addition to killing in on the calls, she also scrapped internet, found information about the prospects from their social history and contextualised her cold calls better than most humans can.

On one call, she requested for an alternate phone number of a prospect as his wife picked up the call. She got the number, and then called on that new number which wasn't even in the database. 

By day 5, she had already paid for herself. 

I had a great conversation with Mahmoud Khodor where I shared how the AI Employees of CozmoX AI (YC W22) are redefining how future work-force would look like. Sharing the link in the comments. Thanks Mahmoud, for the opportunity :)

If you’re looking to unleash exponential growth and bring on an unstoppable closer, DM me. Let’s get Liza on your team.

OpenAI just dropped the biggest bomb in the Agentic world, and thousands of startups are likely to die.

This is the mainstream narrative. It sells, and it elevates every OpenAI launch to a tech apocalypse. But the fact is, the majority of these startups didn't have any moat in the first place, they were thin wrappers on LLMs, riding the AI wave with minimal depth.

The real value isn't in building a UX wrapper on LLMs for automating basic tasks, it's in enterprise-grade orchestration.

In our case at CozmoX AI (YC W22), this was our focus from day one. We built a platform for AI employees, not bots or siloed agents. Our AI employees work like an actual team. They think, coordinate, and execute, just like humans (but faster).

A siloed agent can book your movie ticket, but our AI Employee platform helps you build custom AI Employees who can run your entire sales and support teams, update CRMs, learn and adjust strategy on the fly, and execute on their own, even in regulated sectors like BFSI.

What we built required effort: deep integrations, understanding tech stacks, workflows, domain knowledge, enterprise memory, and context retention. But that's when a moat is built. 

And that's when you stop fearing every OpenAI product drop.

People are looking at AI Employees the wrong way.

I don't understand why there is even a debate between human employees & AI employees. That's like asking if a motorcycle 'replaces' a bicycle. Of course it does, but if you reduce a motorcycle to just a 'faster cycle with an engine,' you've missed the whole point.

A motorcycle's utility goes beyond speeding up commute. It reshapes the cities, it changes how far people can live from they work, it shifts real estate, and it redefine how we think about distance.

The same flawed lens is being applied to AI employees. This isn’t a debate about automation vs. augmentation. The real question is: Why have we tolerated the same broken inefficiencies in sales, collections & customer service for decades?

- 35% of agents leave, taking the context with them 
- What actually works is siloed as tribal knowledge
- Businesses pay same $ for top agents as well as bottom ones
- Employe trainings are reactive, not proactive

Automation via AI employees is just the tip of the iceberg, the real & compounding value is their ability to retain context, being proactive, sharing what works instantly across systems & getting better with every interaction. 

So, don’t judge them only on how “human” they sound. Judge them on how intelligently they think. 

The true value of Voice AI is not in mimicry, it’s in memory, learning, and systems-level intelligence. AI Employees aren't your faster bicycles, they are a whole new level of movement.

Talk to us at CozmoX AI (YC W22), and see what’s possible when you replace pedals with engines, powered by our AI Employees.

"You can not wake up with the same problem as yesterday!"

This one line from Pranav completely changed our perspective on how fast we needed to move to keep up with the growth of CozmoX AI (YC W22).

We’ve been sprinting on the GTM front - fast, focused, and relentless. And it showed in our momentum. But on other fronts, we went too deep. We deliberated & we waited to be sure.

After our chat with Pranav, it clicked that in high-growth startups, the biggest risk isn’t making the wrong decision, it’s indecision. 

If you're building fast, you have to decide faster to keep up with the growth. Whether it’s scaling infra, hiring, entering new markets, or even redefining the problem space itself. Growth doesn’t wait.

As we are preparing for a major milestone in the next few weeks ;) , these are the rockstars we are hiring for CozmoX AI (YC W22):

1) Engineering Leader (Bangalore based) - 8+ years of experience - backend heavy
2) Data and AI Leader (Bangalore based)- 8+ years of experience - python heavy 
3) Staff Fullstack Engineer (Bangalore based)- 5+ years of experience 
4) Head of Customer Excellence (UAE based)- 5+ years of experience
5) Head of Marketing and Growth (geography agnostic)- 5+ years of experience

- Once you go through initial process, we will give you an offer / outcome in 72 working hours. 
- All roles are hands-on execution focused - not managerial. 
- Pay ranges - above market standards + ESOP (0.1% - 0.5%). The ESOPs matter -> due to the major announcement IFYKYK ;) 


Apply here : https://lnkd.in/dnasABnM

Dogfooding our own product got us to $1M ARR in just 12 weeks.

Today, over 90% of our meetings are booked by our AI Employees. They've been shouldering the work since day 1. They make the calls, pitch CozmoX AI (YC W22), and get prospects excited enough to join a demo with our team.

By the time someone talks to us, they’ve already heard the product in action and it worked on them. No extra convincing needed.

Our AI Employees are constantly learning what works, dropping what doesn’t, and compounding the context and intelligence with every conversation. It’s a flywheel: the more they work, the smarter they get, and the faster we grow.

Our conviction in AI Employees led us to pivot from Zywa to CozmoX AI. And now, dogfooding our own tech is fueling our growth engine.

This is my first time building a B2B company, and if we can clock such numbers with the help of our AI Employees, so can you. 

Ping me if you're ready to grow faster.

We just returned from Bangalore after growing our team by over 150% 🚀

Nuha and I spent three intense weeks in the city, interviewing over 90 candidates to build our India team. We landed with zero team members, and left with a powerhouse of 9 amazing folks. The talent density in Bangalore is unreal.

Before this, our founding team, without any prior AI experience, built AI Employees from scratch and scaled it to $1M ARR with just 6 team members. All out of sheer obsession and grit.

Now, with 9 stellar new teammates onboard, we're set to scale CozmoX AI (YC W22) even faster.

If you're someone who doesn't wait for permission and gets obsessed about what you're building, then you're of our kind. Reach out to me, we’ve got a few seats left on the rocketship.

"I can just hire someone for $5 an hour to make cold calls. Why pay for an AI Employee?”

A prospect said this to me recently, and I really love such questions :)

Because they reveal an outdated, incremental mindset in a world that’s shifting exponentially.

Sure, $5-an-hour humans can make cold calls, but they won't be able to compete with an AI Employee that can make thousands of calls in a day, work 24/7, and carry no emotional baggage. Basically, it's an always ON, infinitely scalable employee.

There will always be late adopters, the kind who only upgrade their TV when the remote stops working and spare parts are out of production.

Right now, there’s still an "infinite" supply of cheap labor willing to do cold calls and handle angry customers for $5/hour. But soon, more and more such jobs would handled by AI Employees and gradually, the human workforce would either move up the value chain or find something else to work on. 

And guess who'd be out of business running bloated human capital costs? The ones who'd try to find $5 an hour employees.

We’re in the middle of the biggest technology shift since the internet. The ones who see & embrace it now will dominate tomorrow. And the ones who won't will be the case studies we'll talk about later.

If that sounds uncomfortable, it should. Disruption is never polite, and it doesn’t wait for permission. It rewards those who adapt and buries those who cling to the past.

So, you can either compete on cost, or create value that’s 10x better.

Make something people want and will continue to want.

We pivoted to CozmoX AI (YC W22) AI and achieved $1M ARR with just 6 team members in 12 weeks!

We were building Zywa, a FinTech startup to help Gen Z become smarter with money. Things were picking up, inbound interest was growing, and so were the support queries.

At first, we handled the support ourselves. But as the volume increased, we turned to Voice AI. But what we got was robotic, generic, forgetful, and basically unusable AI.

So, trained the models ourselves & got our 'AI Employee' to handle support & inbound calls.

And then something unexpected happened.

One of our investors called. Not to ask about Zywa, but to ask who his daughter had been speaking with in a Californian accent, he knew everyone in our team. She’d been talking to our AI Employee. When we told him it was our own AI Employee, he asked, “Can you build one for me too? I’ll pay $2,000 a month.”

Both Nuha and I were like, "Whaaaat?"

That was new, our own investor wanted to pay for what we built. But we didn't want celebrate a false positive, so we went to GITEX and presented ourselves as an "AI Employees" platform and got massive attention. We started getting more demand than we could deal with.

That’s when we pivoted. We became CozmoX AI. In just 12 weeks, with a team of 6, we hit $1M ARR.

Today, our AI Employees handle sales, support, and collections for clients across 5 countries.

We didn’t set out to build an AI company, we were just obsessed to solve our own problem better than anyone else could. Sometimes, that’s when real opportunities show up!

Who knew FBI hostage negotiation tactics could boost debt collections?

One of our customers recently trained their Voice AI Employees using negotiation principles from Christopher Voss, former FBI hostage negotiator and author of Never Split The Difference.

Their debt collection rates jumped up by 20%. Looks like Chris Voss might have a future in AI training too :)

Honestly, Nuha and I weren’t surprised. Just like with people, the best-trained AI agents outperform the rest. But that only happens when we stop treating AI as an automation tool and start seeing it as a teammate.

I don’t think people hate talking to AI, they’re mostly agnostic to it. What they hate is talking to bots that lack context, sound robotic, and have zero emotional awareness.

So, the real leap forward isn’t about making AI talk, it’s about helping it understand. Building agents with shared memory, contextual awareness, and emotional intelligence is how we move from “voice bots” to virtual teammates who can hold high-EQ, high-context conversations on their own - to connect, persuade, and deliver results.

Disruption is never quiet, it's never comfortable and it has to be spoken out LOUD! 

I see a lot of AI founders holding their breath when someone asks them - "Will this take away this particular job?" A lot of augmentation vs automation debate also came out of this - we are hesitant in saying this clearly, "Yes, this will reduce your manpower by over 50%."

Ever since we launched CozmoX AI (YC W22), we also came across a lot of these questions and we took a call to be direct, made automation core to our messaging and decided that we won't beat around the bush.

Our customers loved this clarity. So did our team.

We took that message out to the world, literally. 300+ taxis across the UAE helped us share a simple truth: AI Employees are not a someday thing. They’re here. They are active 24/7, they are efficient, they have IQ, they have EQ and they're the best choice to run your contact centers - support, sales, collections... you name it.

And again, UAE loved it :)

Founders, take a stance. Transformation starts with a clear voice, not a quiet one. It’s time to say it out loud.


"There’s no real dogfight in voice AI." 

That's my first realisation after spending a week here in the valley.  The market is massive, underserved, and we’re all just scratching the surface. 

The only way to understand what’s really happening is hitting the field, meeting people and asking questions. That’s how we approached GTM in MENA, find real buyers, listen, and offer solutions.

That's what we've been doing in the SF. 

For example, this week, I walked into a Wells Fargo branch. There's no voice AI, no automation, no AI agents. Just human bandwidth stretched thin. So we pitched them an AI Employee to handle frontline support.

The branch manager listened, then handed us a card, “I’ll connect you to HQ.”

That moment said a lot. Even in the Valley, enterprise workflows are still duct-taped together. Everyone’s running pilots, but very few are scaling. The infra isn’t there. The mental model isn’t there.

Coming from the UAE, we thought we’d be stepping into a hyper-competitive space. Turns out, it’s still early. It's fragmented. Everyone’s still figuring it out.

The dogfight comes later, when delivery becomes predictable, the product moat is gone, and distribution becomes the only moat. Voice AI world is not there yet.

Full circle moment for me.

Four years ago, I left Strategic Gears to start Zywa, which eventually evolved into Cozmo AI. And now, things have truly come full circle. Strategic Gears just signed up with Cozmo AI.

My mentor has become our partner :)

I’m grateful to Nasser, my first mentor, for taking me under his wing back then and for trusting what we’re building today. Everything I learned about building, moving fast, and setting the bar high came from working under him. Your first mentor shapes you more than you realize: how you think, how you execute, and what you believe is possible. Nasser did that for me.

When I left to start something of my own, he wished me luck. Since then, it’s been four years of pure startup hustle: building, pivoting, learning, scaling.

Exciting days ahead.

Major life update - Nuha Hashem and I have moved to the US.

The irony isn't lost on me. Four years ago, I convinced Nuha to leave the US and come build a startup with me in the Middle East. Now we're both back, building where the action is.

A lot has changed since then. The Bay Area has become the epicenter of AI. The talent density is unmatchable, and there’s this contagious energy to build fast and grow faster.

We've been flying back and forth for months, meeting customers and investors. And honestly, it's really evident that the valley is hungrier than ever. 

What’s really amazing is how conversations start here.

Everywhere else, you're explaining why voice AI matters, why orchestration beats isolated agents, why context compounds. Here, that's baseline. People immediately jump to: what are you building? How fast can you ship? What's your moat?

That shift is everything for a startup. You're not fighting uphill battles on the 'why.' You're in the arena where the game is actually being played.

We still have our team in the UAE. We still have customers there. But the next chapter of Cozmo AI has to be written from here.

Our mission stays the same: Build reliable AI Workforce for mission critical regulated industries. Just now, we're building from the epicenter.

If you're in the Bay Area, let's grab coffee!

rows.Post:
My biggest hiring mistake was hiring salespeople at $1M ARR.

When we hit our first million in revenue, I assumed we had a repeatable playbook. So we hired a few AEs expecting them to replicate what I was doing. They struggled, and it wasn’t just their fault.

In AI, things change so fast that you often need to pivot your pitch mid-call. The product evolves, customer objections shift, and positioning needs real-time adjustment.

Founders can do this because they have full context. Delegating sales too early leads to people trying to execute a process that does not actually exist yet. We didn’t have a process. We had a few wins that looked like a pattern but weren’t repeatable.

Looking back, we should have waited until $3 to $5M ARR before handing off sales. That is when the motion stabilizes enough for someone else to run it.

That's why we've been driving our US GTM motion ourselves: 4-5 meetings a day, refining positioning & iterating in real time. And it's paying off, we’ve build a solid enterprise pipeline in the US in this quarter alone.

Restrain the urge to hire until you have a repeatable playbook and not just revenue milestones.

One of our customers started using Cozmo AI to cut headcount. They ended up hiring 4 new people instead, and they were excited about it.

They had thousands of B2C contacts they had written off as dead data. They uploaded them to our AI employee, Liza, expecting maybe a few qualified leads. But Liza managed to call those contacts and surfaced hundreds of opportunities their team had completely missed. Those high-intent prospects were buried as zombie contacts.

Within 2 months, they closed 3 deals and their pipeline kept growing. So they hired more people and built an entire team around it.

These were our early days, and I recall them getting panicked when we had downtime, as their new hires couldn't operate without the AI-qualified pipeline. The business line they had just built was completely dependent on AI doing the qualification work.

This is why I keep saying that using AI as a cost optimization tool is underplaying its potential. The real use is building leverage. In this case, the AI employees revealed opportunities they could not see before.

We spent last week at AWS re:Invent in Vegas, and it was one of the best-organized technical conferences I've attended.

What really struck me wasn’t the product announcements or the keynotes. It was a candid message from AWS leadership that genuinely reframed how I think about infrastructure businesses.

They said, "We don't care if you compete with our products. We want to work with category winners. If your customer base grows AWS compute, we'll support you, even if you're competing with Amazon Connect."

That's a fundamentally different philosophy than platform companies like Salesforce or Microsoft, who guard their territory and lock you into their ecosystem. AWS knows that they can't predict which application layer dominates in AI. So they don't try. They enable everyone and let the market decide. 

Their moat isn't features. It's neutrality. 

By staying neutral and supporting any company that drives compute, AWS positions itself as the foundational layer of an expanding AI economy. If the category grows, they grow, regardless of who wins on top..

When infrastructure companies stop picking winners and simply enable the game, they become the house that always wins.

I'm in Vegas this week for AWS re:Invent. 

We've attended 7 events in the last 12 days, and this is where most startups fall flat in these events, they are still indexing over demos, or showing how 'human' their AI is, or how they've solved for latency. 

Enterprises treat that as baseline now, the real game now is showing that you can survive the production traffic (calls, emails, tickets), and your product can really learn & build their internal context, not just follow scripts. 

So, Alok and I have switched gears, we stopped demoing our product weeks ago. Now we spend time in understanding the context of enterprises, their workflows, their pain points, their tech stack and their actual reality. Because that's what makes you part of the top 5% pool where deployments actually work.

If you're at re:Invent, reach out to me or Alok and let's schedule a 30‑minute session. We'd love to get to know you better and show you how our AI Employees are graduating from demos to outcomes.

Everyone told us the Bay Area would be a bloodbath. It's not.

We expected hyper-competition, saturated pipelines, and customers drowning in Voice AI pitches. What we found instead: massive white space.

We've walked into banks & insurance companies. Most have zero AI deployed. Some of them tried and got burnt. They did failed pilots with players who over-promised and under-delivered. Now they're cautious, building "AI councils" and strategy decks instead of shipping.

And this is the part people miss: market maturity is not the same as market penetration.

Yes, the Bay Area is ahead in understanding. People here know why voice matters, why agents need memory, why orchestration beats isolated agents, why context compounds. But understanding is not equal to deployment.

That gap between "we understand this" and "we've deployed this" is where the entire opportunity sits. Not just for us, but for any player who can go beyond slides and actually deliver outcomes.

Most enterprises are still figuring it out. Once bitten, twice shy. They want proof, not promises and demos.

The dogfight everyone warned us about is not here yet. The market is massive, underserved, and wide open for players who can actually deliver.

Full circle moment for me.

Four years ago, I left Strategic Gears to start Zywa, which eventually evolved into Cozmo AI. And now, things have truly come full circle. Strategic Gears just signed up with Cozmo AI.

My mentor has become our partner :)

I’m grateful to Nasser, my first mentor, for taking me under his wing back then and for trusting what we’re building today. Everything I learned about building, moving fast, and setting the bar high came from working under him. Your first mentor shapes you more than you realize: how you think, how you execute, and what you believe is possible. Nasser did that for me.

When I left to start something of my own, he wished me luck. Since then, it’s been four years of pure startup hustle: building, pivoting, learning, scaling.

Exciting days ahead.

Sponsoring TechCrunch Disrupt this year was the best decision to kickstart our US GTM.

The energy and conversations were amazing… you see the photos, but being there is something else entirely.

Everywhere I went, people were deep in discussions about AI agents, evals, guardrails, and what’s real vs what’s hype.

We had back to back conversations at our booth, and we ended up filling our calendars for a couple of weeks ahead. A few of those chats are already turning into real opportunities.

It was evident that if we’re serious about building at the pace AI is moving, we need to be here, at the epicenter of AI action.

The funny thing is, we’d been planning to move to the US for months. But between customer deployments, product launches, and the daily startup chaos, it kept getting pushed by “one more week.”

So, we finally decided to give ourselves a deadline: let’s sponsor TechCrunch Disrupt. That became our line in the sand. From that point, everything started falling into place.

And here we are, Cozmo AI now has a US office 🇺🇸

We’re still growing in the UAE, and still supporting our customers there, but this next chapter is where we double down on our mission of building the Voice AI Workforce for regulated enterprises.

TechCrunch Disrupt turned out to be the catalyst we needed to unlock the next phase of Cozmo AI.

Super excited for what’s ahead 🚀

Cozmo AI, Nuha Hashem, Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna

"We're officially releasing our enterprise-grade, Arabic-speaking AI Employees today at GITEX, powered by our Agent Protocol Engine (APE).

The most valuable business interactions still happen over the phone. Yet most are handled by outdated IVRs, call centers, or teams that can’t scale. We’re replacing that with AI Employees who can think, speak, and act; always-on, infinitely scalable, and fluent in 80+ languages including Khaleeji and Masri Arabic.

This week at GITEX 2025, our team is showcasing what we have achieved in the last 9 months with multiple enterprises in 4 key areas:
- Real-time reasoning AI Employees for banking, financial services and insurance (BFSI)
- Voice infrastructure built for enterprise scale (10,000+ calls/sec)
- Evals, customizable context engineering, and 500+ guardrails per industry 
- Agentic AI that augments, not replaces your people

If you’re building or leading an enterprise, talk with Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna in: A) Gitex - Cyber Valley - hall 25 booth 14, or B) Expand North Star - hall 4 booth 2 and discover our enterprise-grade, AI Employees (who also speak in Arabic) at GITEX/Expand North Star.

Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric.

Everyone is underestimating the AI Agent market.

Most people still think in straight lines: one human = one agent. But that’s the wrong lens.

The real unlock is parallelism. Imagine this:

- Instead of one coding agent struggling through a huge codebase, multiple agents write, review, test, and refactor different parts, simultaneously.
- Instead of one analyst agent piecing together research, dozens scan diverse sources, validate facts, and summarize insights in parallel.
- Instead of one UI agent handling user requests serially, a network of agents divides and conquers, dynamically assigning tasks and updating strategies in real time.

As LLM inference costs drop and architectures improve, this becomes both technically feasible and economically attractive.

Tasks that once took teams of humans (or weren’t feasible at all) can now collapse into hours. Entire teams and workflows can now be augmented, accelerated, and scaled beyond human limits.

This opens up markets that were never addressable before.

Today, most still see agents as productivity tools, ways to get things done faster. But their potential is much deeper, they change how we think about automation, orchestration, and work itself.

That’s why we provide AI Employees, not just agents. Employees operate with context, memory, and accountability, and they reshape how work actually gets done by executing multiple tasks, not just one.

The TAM for AI Agents isn’t limited by headcount, it's limited by imagination. So, the next breakthroughs won’t come from replacing humans, they’ll come from reimagining what’s possible when work itself is redefined.

Everyone's hyped about AgentKit revolutionizing AI agents, but let’s talk about what it doesn't solve, especially for Voice AI.

AgentKit is a big step forward for agent frameworks. It simplifies orchestration, evaluation, and tool use. But Voice AI has its own layer of complexity that no SDK can abstract away yet.

Voice interfaces aren’t just chatbots with speech wrappers. They’re real-time systems with constraints that text doesn’t have. They need: 
- Low-latency ASR/TTS
- Real-time interrupt handling
- Robust error recovery
- Natural turn-taking
...none of which AgentKit addresses directly.

Yes, it’s great for workflow orchestration, tool use, and evaluation. But hardest problems aren’t in the logic orchestration. They’re in milliseconds, in how fast speech gets processed, how interruptions are handled, and how context survives over multiple turns.

That’s the layer where CozmoX AI (YC W22) lives. Our moat isn’t just the agent logic, it’s the voice infrastructure that keeps real conversations coherent, contextual, and human-like, across millions of calls.

So while AgentKit accelerates reasoning, the opportunity now is in building the missing pieces:
- Voice-native agents
- Low-latency speech pipelines
- Better UX for spoken interaction

AgentKit is a foundation, but voice needs its own playbook. And that’s exactly what we’re writing at CozmoX AI.

Leverage > Cost Cutting

If you’re installing AI Employees just to 'optimise' for headcount, you’re underplaying their potential.

Cost optimisation is a finite game. You can cut costs by 90%, and hit the cap.

But leverage creation is an infinite game; every insight compounds into the next dollar, the next market, the next product line.

At CozmoX AI (YC W22), 60% of our customers didn’t come to replace agents, they came to out-sell and out-collect their competition.

- A telco routed upsell calls to our AI Sales Employee; ARPU rose 14% in six weeks.
- A logistics player boosted their recovery of outstanding dues by 3x without extra payroll by leveraging out our AI Collections Employee.
- Every learning from one region, let's say Riyadh, propagates to Nairobi in minutes. This would take off-sites, training sessions, and hundreds of thousands of dollars in the real world.

Yes, a lean P&L looks great this quarter. But leverage rewrites next year’s forecast, and the one after that.

Because it’s never the AI that sets your ceiling, it’s what you choose to multiply with it. And that starts with the one rule that never changes - make something people want :)

Last week, I got into a car accident. 

Fortunately, I walked away with minor injuries. But that was a wake-up call I didn't know I needed. 

I’ve barely spoken to my parents since I started building, first Zywa, and now CozmoX. I’ve been in constant “go” mode, always heads down, building, pushing, telling myself I’ll call them next week.

But that “next week” kept slipping.

After the accident, the first people I called were my parents and my brother. Only after hearing their voices, I could think straight.

Moments like these remind us what and who matters the most, and that we need to take a pause and talk to the people who love us unconditionally, who don't care about our revenue, funding, or traction. They ask simple things: 
- Did you eat?
- Are you sleeping well?

As founders, we often take our families for granted. We put them on the back burner, until a moment like this forces us to wake up and makes us realise that no metric matters more than your people. If you’ve been grinding nonstop, maybe this is your cue to pause and call home. 

You don't have to wait for a crisis to make time for your people.

What's common in all these companies? They’re all gateways into synthetic data. And the space is hot, everyone is talking about synthetic data. 

And sure, it’s powerful. It fills gaps, covers edge cases, and makes models look smarter than they really are. 

But over-reliance on synthetic data is where products fail. Because at the end of the day, synthetic is a booster, not the anchor. The moment you forget that, your product starts drifting into fiction:

- Support bots that sound polite but can’t calm an actually furious customer.
- Sales agents that ace transcripts but collapse the second a prospect goes off-script.
- Vision models that nail fake images but fail in the messy physical world.

That’s how you end up with a product that wins demos but loses in reality.

The solution to this is simple: seed with real data, even if that's small, boost it with synthetic data, and keep pulling back your models to reality whenever they drift away.

Because customers don’t care about your training tricks. They care if your product works in the real world - that's what gets the sales done, calms an angry customer or ensures debt is collected.

You either die as an AI agent, or live long enough to become an AI employee.

AI Agents are great, but they are territorial. They are good at siloed tasks like sending an email, making a call, updating a CRM field, but not at owning an outcome end to end.

AI employees, on the other hand, are outcome-driven. They don’t care about defending “their task.” They care about hitting the target like revenue closed, tickets resolved, payments collected.

Take Alex, our AI Debt Collector. His job is, as the name suggests, collecting debt. Now, that involves doing cold calls, updating CRM, gathering context, scheduling follow up calls, and consistently learning what type of calls drive better collections.

In one case, he called a borrower, but reached the spouse. So, he asked for an alternate number, and then made the call on a number that wasn’t even in the CRM. That’s what outcome ownership looks like.

We won’t see a true AI-human hybrid workforce until AI agents graduate into AI employees. And that shift depends on orchestration, the brain that turns siloed tasks into one coherent outcome.

Agents are like instruments, but an employee is the band that plays in sync to deliver the performance in a concert. And enterprises don’t want more instruments lying around, they want the concert.

And here’s the team with Cooke, our Chief Vibes Officer, building the band that will define the future of work.

Last month, we realised that we were busy without moving faster. 

We had tried building our team across geographies to attract top talent, regardless of location. On paper, it looked ideal. In practice, it slowed us down.

Our Slack threads kept growing while our speed of shipping and implementations struggled. We realised that in early stages proximity and white-boarding together solves for the need of internal alignment every now and then. 

So we made a call, either build the early team where we are (UAE) or where our customers are (US, UK, UAE). If someone is exceptional, we relocate them. Otherwise we keep the center of gravity tight and focused.

So, we moved our team to UAE and our productivity skyrocketed. And it's not about hours in the office, it's about shoulder taps, five‑minute whiteboard fixes, and the kind of momentum that only happens when the people who ship sit next to the people who sell. 

I believe, remote work can win at scale, but in the early stages proximity compounds. You retain context, argue less and ship more.

We will soon have AI Employees driving Sales as well as Procurement end to end.

They'll find fitment, negotiate contracts, and then loop in decisions makers for the final sign off, while giving them the full context.

Today, procurement teams spend enormously high number of hours in finding fitments, running through a checklist, getting RFPs filled, reviewing documents. I believe all this can be automated by voice, there can be an AI Employee assisting buyers and an AI employee assisting sellers who'd filter the noise out so that decision makers can spend their time on most aligned partners.

In the near future, we will move beyond automating tasks, and look at AI for automating entire roles to drive outcomes. And with AI Employees embedded across the value chain, business functions will shift from coordination and execution to strategy and creativity.

Here’s why context, not headcount, is the future of compounding growth.

Recently, a customer win in Riyadh helped our AI employee close a deal in Nairobi just an hour later.

Alex, our AI debt collector, had a successful call in Saudi Arabia, and within the hour, the same tone and nudges were used in Kenya as well. The prospects agreed to pay, right there on the call.

Humans typically share wins during meetings, off-sites, or quarterly sync-ups. AI, however, can share wins in minutes. This is the true power of building a system of shared memory that actually gets used and keeps compounding.

How we run it:
- Every successful rebuttal, policy clarification, and exception path becomes a reusable pattern.
- It is not a memo. It is something the next conversation can use immediately.
- As a result, best practices compound continuously, driving win rates higher.

The old way to scale was headcount: train, retrain, and hope that best practices get picked up. The new way is through context. Capture the insight once and push it across regions instantly.

You do not need more people to avoid repeating the same mistake. You need a system that moves knowledge at network speed.

Headcount growth is linear, and context growth is exponential. It is really not even a choice anymore to go ahead with AI employees.

AI Employees are already managing humans.

Last week our AI Employee assigned calls to seven human reps. It was NOT a demo, it was on live traffic.

It read intent, checked capacity, pushed tasks, and flagged escalations, without any ego or hallway politics. Two shifts later the human lead pinged me asking for the AI’s dashboard so he could plan coverage. That was the moment it clicked. Not only we were augmenting or replacing agents, we were re‑wiring even their management.

The strategy is simple. 
- We define the goal for the day. Reduce average handle time, increase right‑party connects or chase a collections milestone. 
- The AI takes the playbook and routes work to the right human at the right moment. 
- It adaptively schedules callbacks, senses sentiment, and escalates only when a human’s judgment can move the needle.

We even saw a quiet shift in accountability. Instead of managers chasing metrics at the end of the week, they now look at our dashboard on real time. The bottlenecks don't hide anymore, coaching has gotten precise, and the best calls get wired in to improve overall output.

I get asked if this threatens people. It does, if they still want to operate in the legacy way.

The team that wins next year is the team that treats AI not just as an employee or a tool enabling them, but also as the floor manager.

The best talent does not follow a single S-curve, as it eventually saturates. They go through multiple S-curves, each one compounding on the last.

When we pivoted from Zywa to CozmoX AI (YC W22), everything changed, except one thing: the need to be tenacious, and adaptive.

The problem we set out to solve was completely different. It demanded new skills, deeper domain expertise, and a whole new mindset. We weren’t just entering a new market, we were stepping into a new industry, with a new playbook (and often, writing it ourselves).

But instead of rebuilding the team, we doubled down on our people.
- ⁠Our iOS engineer became a backend engineer.
- ⁠Our Android engineer reinvented himself as an AI engineer.
- ⁠Our backend engineer set his sights on becoming an ML architect.

Even Nuha and I went through our own crash course, learning the ropes of AI, VAD architecture, Ci/CD pipelines, B2B sales, and how to navigate enterprise decision cycles.

Startups often emphasize deep, vertical expertise, and that’s important. But in our experience, there’s nothing more powerful than gritty generalists, people who are willing to unlearn and relearn everything they know, again and again.

You can't pay your way into PMF or enterprise adoption.

I've built a consumer-facing business before, and now I'm building an enterprise-facing one. With end consumers, you can run campaigns, offer promos, burn money, and change behavior over time. Think Uber, DoorDash.

But enterprises are not impulse buyers, and I’ve learned that you can’t buy your way into their workflows. There’s no way to manufacture urgency if you're not solving real pain, and there's no growth hack to become 'mission critical' for enterprises.

I’ve seen polished demos go nowhere, even when the board was pushing an AI mandate. If the product didn’t solve something painful, it got a polite “no thanks.”

And I’ve seen the opposite too, budgets unlocked overnight when a product hit a real nerve.

Ironically, the only "shortcut" I’ve found to PMF in enterprise is doing the slow, unscalable work of sitting with early adopters, figuring out what’s broken, and co-creating with them.

We grew CozmoX AI (YC W22) to over $1M ARR in 12 weeks by ignoring the generic pattern-matching advice we kept hearing.

We fired 4 customers last week, 5% of our revenue. It's one of the hardest things a founder has to do. But here's why it's essential:

In the early days, you say yes to anyone willing to pay. Every dollar helps you survive, and every logo feels like progress. But beyond a point, that survival instinct becomes a growth bottleneck.

Last week, we reviewed usage across a few of our earliest accounts. These were teams that had gone live, but with almost no call volumes, no urgency to scale, no dedicated reps, and no real push for outcomes.

So, we made a framework and decided to filter them out:
- <$20K/month spend = dropped
- No scale = dropped
- Outside our vertical thesis = dropped
- Not going ahead with deeper integrations and workflows = dropped

Honestly, if we hadn’t done it, they would’ve churned anyway. We just went first and ended the relationship with clarity and respect.

At our stage, we don't need passive customers, we need design partners, growth catalysts, and teams that scale with us.

Founders hate doing this because of the sunk cost fallacy, months of effort already invested in the relationship. But the reality is, such relationships are just coexistence without mutual growth. And that’s a drag on both sides.

Retention doesn’t mean keeping everyone. It means choosing who you want to grow with. And not all churn is bad, some of it is just you clearing space for the customers who are actually ready to win.

Founders set a very high bar for what would be 'acceptable' to their customers, and that obsession kills more startups than bad ideas, lack of funding, or competition.

Because it delays reality.

We dodged that 'perfection trap' by starting to sell before fully building our AI Employees. That gave us the right signals to define what a minimum lovable product actually looked like for our customers.

While we wanted to chase near-perfect latency in our AI Employees, our customers didn’t really care about that level of perfection. They cared about the outcomes our AI Employees delivered.

What our first customer signed up for was very different from what we have today. But that early customer has stayed with us, and they’ve loved seeing the product get better every week. That visible progress built more trust than any “perfect” product ever could.

The market doesn’t reward perfection. It rewards momentum.

Your obsession with perfection is just a disguised fear of rejection. Let the market humble you (or celebrate you) quickly while you iterate your product toward PMF.

We just sunset our entire Account Management team, not because customer success matters less, but because the old playbook can't keep pace with the speed of AI-led transformation.

Traditional account management was built for planned delivery, following a scope, managing hand-offs, quarterly check-ins, annual renewals. That works when one-size-fits-all SaaS is acceptable.

But that era is over. Today, enterprises want AI that understands their data, their workflows, their stakes & their environment. And the only way to deliver that is to move away from planned delivery to embedded execution. 

So, we replaced AMs with FDTs, Forward Deployed Teams. 

They are hybrid operators, engineers who sit inside customer environments, observe real workflows, identify friction, build or integrate solutions and co-create deployments that guarantee outcomes. Basically, they pair contextual empathy of customers with our engineering abilities. 

FDT is now the heart of customer success at CozmoX AI (YC W22). I’m betting more AI startups will follow, because the renewal driver has shifted. It's not licences sold anymore, it's the outcomes delivered.

OpenAI just dropped the biggest bomb in the Agentic world, and thousands of startups are likely to die.

This is the mainstream narrative. It sells, and it elevates every OpenAI launch to a tech apocalypse. But the fact is, the majority of these startups didn't have any moat in the first place, they were thin wrappers on LLMs, riding the AI wave with minimal depth.

The real value isn't in building a UX wrapper on LLMs for automating basic tasks, it's in enterprise-grade orchestration.

In our case at CozmoX AI (YC W22), this was our focus from day one. We built a platform for AI employees, not bots or siloed agents. Our AI employees work like an actual team. They think, coordinate, and execute, just like humans (but faster).

A siloed agent can book your movie ticket, but our AI Employee platform helps you build custom AI Employees who can run your entire sales and support teams, update CRMs, learn and adjust strategy on the fly, and execute on their own, even in regulated sectors like BFSI.

What we built required effort: deep integrations, understanding tech stacks, workflows, domain knowledge, enterprise memory, and context retention. But that's when a moat is built. 

And that's when you stop fearing every OpenAI product drop.

People are looking at AI Employees the wrong way.

I don't understand why there is even a debate between human employees & AI employees. That's like asking if a motorcycle 'replaces' a bicycle. Of course it does, but if you reduce a motorcycle to just a 'faster cycle with an engine,' you've missed the whole point.

A motorcycle's utility goes beyond speeding up commute. It reshapes the cities, it changes how far people can live from they work, it shifts real estate, and it redefine how we think about distance.

The same flawed lens is being applied to AI employees. This isn’t a debate about automation vs. augmentation. The real question is: Why have we tolerated the same broken inefficiencies in sales, collections & customer service for decades?

- 35% of agents leave, taking the context with them 
- What actually works is siloed as tribal knowledge
- Businesses pay same $ for top agents as well as bottom ones
- Employe trainings are reactive, not proactive

Automation via AI employees is just the tip of the iceberg, the real & compounding value is their ability to retain context, being proactive, sharing what works instantly across systems & getting better with every interaction. 

So, don’t judge them only on how “human” they sound. Judge them on how intelligently they think. 

The true value of Voice AI is not in mimicry, it’s in memory, learning, and systems-level intelligence. AI Employees aren't your faster bicycles, they are a whole new level of movement.

Talk to us at CozmoX AI (YC W22), and see what’s possible when you replace pedals with engines, powered by our AI Employees.

"You can not wake up with the same problem as yesterday!"

This one line from Pranav completely changed our perspective on how fast we needed to move to keep up with the growth of CozmoX AI (YC W22).

We’ve been sprinting on the GTM front - fast, focused, and relentless. And it showed in our momentum. But on other fronts, we went too deep. We deliberated & we waited to be sure.

After our chat with Pranav, it clicked that in high-growth startups, the biggest risk isn’t making the wrong decision, it’s indecision. 

If you're building fast, you have to decide faster to keep up with the growth. Whether it’s scaling infra, hiring, entering new markets, or even redefining the problem space itself. Growth doesn’t wait.

As we are preparing for a major milestone in the next few weeks ;) , these are the rockstars we are hiring for CozmoX AI (YC W22):

1) Engineering Leader (Bangalore based) - 8+ years of experience - backend heavy
2) Data and AI Leader (Bangalore based)- 8+ years of experience - python heavy 
3) Staff Fullstack Engineer (Bangalore based)- 5+ years of experience 
4) Head of Customer Excellence (UAE based)- 5+ years of experience
5) Head of Marketing and Growth (geography agnostic)- 5+ years of experience

- Once you go through initial process, we will give you an offer / outcome in 72 working hours. 
- All roles are hands-on execution focused - not managerial. 
- Pay ranges - above market standards + ESOP (0.1% - 0.5%). The ESOPs matter -> due to the major announcement IFYKYK ;) 


Apply here : https://lnkd.in/dnasABnM

Dogfooding our own product got us to $1M ARR in just 12 weeks.

Today, over 90% of our meetings are booked by our AI Employees. They've been shouldering the work since day 1. They make the calls, pitch CozmoX AI (YC W22), and get prospects excited enough to join a demo with our team.

By the time someone talks to us, they’ve already heard the product in action and it worked on them. No extra convincing needed.

Our AI Employees are constantly learning what works, dropping what doesn’t, and compounding the context and intelligence with every conversation. It’s a flywheel: the more they work, the smarter they get, and the faster we grow.

Our conviction in AI Employees led us to pivot from Zywa to CozmoX AI. And now, dogfooding our own tech is fueling our growth engine.

This is my first time building a B2B company, and if we can clock such numbers with the help of our AI Employees, so can you. 

Ping me if you're ready to grow faster.

We just returned from Bangalore after growing our team by over 150% 🚀

Nuha and I spent three intense weeks in the city, interviewing over 90 candidates to build our India team. We landed with zero team members, and left with a powerhouse of 9 amazing folks. The talent density in Bangalore is unreal.

Before this, our founding team, without any prior AI experience, built AI Employees from scratch and scaled it to $1M ARR with just 6 team members. All out of sheer obsession and grit.

Now, with 9 stellar new teammates onboard, we're set to scale CozmoX AI (YC W22) even faster.

If you're someone who doesn't wait for permission and gets obsessed about what you're building, then you're of our kind. Reach out to me, we’ve got a few seats left on the rocketship.

"I can just hire someone for $5 an hour to make cold calls. Why pay for an AI Employee?”

A prospect said this to me recently, and I really love such questions :)

Because they reveal an outdated, incremental mindset in a world that’s shifting exponentially.

Sure, $5-an-hour humans can make cold calls, but they won't be able to compete with an AI Employee that can make thousands of calls in a day, work 24/7, and carry no emotional baggage. Basically, it's an always ON, infinitely scalable employee.

There will always be late adopters, the kind who only upgrade their TV when the remote stops working and spare parts are out of production.

Right now, there’s still an "infinite" supply of cheap labor willing to do cold calls and handle angry customers for $5/hour. But soon, more and more such jobs would handled by AI Employees and gradually, the human workforce would either move up the value chain or find something else to work on. 

And guess who'd be out of business running bloated human capital costs? The ones who'd try to find $5 an hour employees.

We’re in the middle of the biggest technology shift since the internet. The ones who see & embrace it now will dominate tomorrow. And the ones who won't will be the case studies we'll talk about later.

If that sounds uncomfortable, it should. Disruption is never polite, and it doesn’t wait for permission. It rewards those who adapt and buries those who cling to the past.

So, you can either compete on cost, or create value that’s 10x better.

Make something people want and will continue to want.

We pivoted to CozmoX AI (YC W22) AI and achieved $1M ARR with just 6 team members in 12 weeks!

We were building Zywa, a FinTech startup to help Gen Z become smarter with money. Things were picking up, inbound interest was growing, and so were the support queries.

At first, we handled the support ourselves. But as the volume increased, we turned to Voice AI. But what we got was robotic, generic, forgetful, and basically unusable AI.

So, trained the models ourselves & got our 'AI Employee' to handle support & inbound calls.

And then something unexpected happened.

One of our investors called. Not to ask about Zywa, but to ask who his daughter had been speaking with in a Californian accent, he knew everyone in our team. She’d been talking to our AI Employee. When we told him it was our own AI Employee, he asked, “Can you build one for me too? I’ll pay $2,000 a month.”

Both Nuha and I were like, "Whaaaat?"

That was new, our own investor wanted to pay for what we built. But we didn't want celebrate a false positive, so we went to GITEX and presented ourselves as an "AI Employees" platform and got massive attention. We started getting more demand than we could deal with.

That’s when we pivoted. We became CozmoX AI. In just 12 weeks, with a team of 6, we hit $1M ARR.

Today, our AI Employees handle sales, support, and collections for clients across 5 countries.

We didn’t set out to build an AI company, we were just obsessed to solve our own problem better than anyone else could. Sometimes, that’s when real opportunities show up!
Difference between good and great 💪🏻

Who knew FBI hostage negotiation tactics could boost debt collections?

One of our customers recently trained their Voice AI Employees using negotiation principles from Christopher Voss, former FBI hostage negotiator and author of Never Split The Difference.

Their debt collection rates jumped up by 20%. Looks like Chris Voss might have a future in AI training too :)

Honestly, Nuha and I weren’t surprised. Just like with people, the best-trained AI agents outperform the rest. But that only happens when we stop treating AI as an automation tool and start seeing it as a teammate.

I don’t think people hate talking to AI, they’re mostly agnostic to it. What they hate is talking to bots that lack context, sound robotic, and have zero emotional awareness.

So, the real leap forward isn’t about making AI talk, it’s about helping it understand. Building agents with shared memory, contextual awareness, and emotional intelligence is how we move from “voice bots” to virtual teammates who can hold high-EQ, high-context conversations on their own - to connect, persuade, and deliver results.

Disruption is never quiet, it's never comfortable and it has to be spoken out LOUD! 

I see a lot of AI founders holding their breath when someone asks them - "Will this take away this particular job?" A lot of augmentation vs automation debate also came out of this - we are hesitant in saying this clearly, "Yes, this will reduce your manpower by over 50%."

Ever since we launched CozmoX AI (YC W22), we also came across a lot of these questions and we took a call to be direct, made automation core to our messaging and decided that we won't beat around the bush.

Our customers loved this clarity. So did our team.

We took that message out to the world, literally. 300+ taxis across the UAE helped us share a simple truth: AI Employees are not a someday thing. They’re here. They are active 24/7, they are efficient, they have IQ, they have EQ and they're the best choice to run your contact centers - support, sales, collections... you name it.

And again, UAE loved it :)

Founders, take a stance. Transformation starts with a clear voice, not a quiet one. It’s time to say it out loud.

"There’s no real dogfight in voice AI." 

That's my first realisation after spending a week here in the valley.  The market is massive, underserved, and we’re all just scratching the surface. 

The only way to understand what’s really happening is hitting the field, meeting people and asking questions. That’s how we approached GTM in MENA, find real buyers, listen, and offer solutions.

That's what we've been doing in the SF. 

For example, this week, I walked into a Wells Fargo branch. There's no voice AI, no automation, no AI agents. Just human bandwidth stretched thin. So we pitched them an AI Employee to handle frontline support.

The branch manager listened, then handed us a card, “I’ll connect you to HQ.”

That moment said a lot. Even in the Valley, enterprise workflows are still duct-taped together. Everyone’s running pilots, but very few are scaling. The infra isn’t there. The mental model isn’t there.

Coming from the UAE, we thought we’d be stepping into a hyper-competitive space. Turns out, it’s still early. It's fragmented. Everyone’s still figuring it out.

The dogfight comes later, when delivery becomes predictable, the product moat is gone, and distribution becomes the only moat. Voice AI world is not there yet.

Full circle moment for me.

Four years ago, I left Strategic Gears to start Zywa, which eventually evolved into Cozmo AI. And now, things have truly come full circle. Strategic Gears just signed up with Cozmo AI.

My mentor has become our partner :)

I’m grateful to Nasser, my first mentor, for taking me under his wing back then and for trusting what we’re building today. Everything I learned about building, moving fast, and setting the bar high came from working under him. Your first mentor shapes you more than you realize: how you think, how you execute, and what you believe is possible. Nasser did that for me.

When I left to start something of my own, he wished me luck. Since then, it’s been four years of pure startup hustle: building, pivoting, learning, scaling.

Exciting days ahead.

Major life update - Nuha Hashem and I have moved to the US.

The irony isn't lost on me. Four years ago, I convinced Nuha to leave the US and come build a startup with me in the Middle East. Now we're both back, building where the action is.

A lot has changed since then. The Bay Area has become the epicenter of AI. The talent density is unmatchable, and there’s this contagious energy to build fast and grow faster.

We've been flying back and forth for months, meeting customers and investors. And honestly, it's really evident that the valley is hungrier than ever. 

What’s really amazing is how conversations start here.

Everywhere else, you're explaining why voice AI matters, why orchestration beats isolated agents, why context compounds. Here, that's baseline. People immediately jump to: what are you building? How fast can you ship? What's your moat?

That shift is everything for a startup. You're not fighting uphill battles on the 'why.' You're in the arena where the game is actually being played.

We still have our team in the UAE. We still have customers there. But the next chapter of Cozmo AI has to be written from here.

Our mission stays the same: Build reliable AI Workforce for mission critical regulated industries. Just now, we're building from the epicenter.

If you're in the Bay Area, let's grab coffee!

"There’s no real dogfight in voice AI."

The only way to understand what’s really happening is by hitting the field, meeting people, and asking questions. That’s how we approached GTM in MENA: find real buyers, listen, and offer solutions.

That's what we've been doing in SF.

For example, this week, I walked into a Wells Fargo branch. There's no voice AI, no automation, no AI agents. Just human bandwidth stretched thin. So we pitched them an AI employee to handle frontline support.

The branch manager listened, then handed us a card: “I’ll connect you to HQ.”

That moment said a lot. Even in the Valley, enterprise workflows are still duct-taped together. Everyone’s running pilots, but very few are scaling. The infra isn’t there. The mental model isn’t there.

Coming from the UAE, we thought we’d be stepping into a hyper-competitive space. Turns out, it’s still early. It's fragmented. Everyone’s still figuring it out.

The dogfight comes later, when delivery becomes predictable, the product moat is gone, and distribution becomes the only moat. The voice AI world is not there yet.

    {
      "id": "style_open_bold_claim",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Opens with a flat bold claim that commits before hedging. No qualifiers, no setup \u2014 the opener IS the thesis. Tension created immediately.",
      "examples": [
        "My biggest hiring mistake was hiring salespeople at $1M ARR.",
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric.",
        "There's no real dogfight in voice AI."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Gentle introductions or hedged claims",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_number_drop",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Drops a specific number or metric in the first 8 words to signal credibility and create immediate pattern interrupt.",
      "examples": [
        "$1M ARR in 12 weeks with 6 people.",
        "14% ARPU increase in six weeks.",
        "7 events in 12 days."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Vague claims or percentages without context",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_contradiction",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Opens with a statement that appears contradictory or impossible, then resolves it through the post. Creates cognitive tension.",
      "examples": [
        "Everyone told us the Bay Area would be a bloodbath. It's not.",
        "AI Employees are already managing humans.",
        "One of our customers started using Cozmo AI to cut headcount. They ended up hiring 4 new people instead."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Linear cause-and-effect openers",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_question_buried",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Buries an implicit question in the first line rather than asking it directly. Reader must engage to answer it.",
      "examples": [
        "Full circle moment for me.",
        "Major life update.",
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Direct rhetorical questions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_scene_drop",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Opens mid-scene with physical detail. Reader is dropped into the action without preamble.",
      "examples": [
        "I walked into a Wells Fargo branch.",
        "Last week I got into a car accident.",
        "We spent last week at AWS re:Invent in Vegas."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Abstract setup or context-setting paragraphs",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_confession",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Opens with a personal admission or mistake. Creates vulnerability and authority simultaneously.",
      "examples": [
        "My biggest hiring mistake...",
        "I've barely spoken to my parents since I started building.",
        "Last month, we realised that we were busy without moving faster."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Defensive or qualified admissions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_single_word_or_phrase",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Uses an ultra-short opener (1-5 words) as a hard stop before the first full sentence. Creates rhythm break.",
      "examples": [
        "Full circle moment for me.",
        "Major life update.",
        "Leverage > Cost Cutting"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Complete sentence openers",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_status_announcement",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",
      "description": "Opens with a milestone or status update stated flatly, then unpacks the significance.",
      "examples": [

        "We just sunset our entire Account Management team.",
        "We are hiring 7 engineers in the next 7 days."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Framing announcements with excessive context",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_open_provocative_disagreement",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "opening",

    {
      "id": "style_close_principle",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "Distills the entire post argument into a single transferable principle. Not a CTA, not a summary \u2014 a principle the reader can take away.",
      "examples": [

        "When infrastructure companies stop picking winners and simply enable the game, they become the house that always wins.",

    {
      "id": "style_close_open_question",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "Ends with an unanswered question that hangs in the air. Not asking the reader to answer \u2014 leaving them with something to think about.",
      "examples": [

      "id": "style_close_call_to_action_disguised",
