    {
      "id": "style_close_echo_opener",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "The closing echoes the opener \u2014 a word, a phrase, or the structural logic of the opening returns at the end to create resonance.",
      "examples": [
        "Full circle moment. Exciting days ahead.",
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric. Even if it takes years.",
        "They hired more people and built an entire team around it."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Conclusions that introduce new ideas",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_close_forward_looking",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "Ends by pointing forward \u2014 what happens next, what this unlocks, what comes after. Not a CTA, but a momentum statement.",
      "examples": [
        "Exciting days ahead.",
        "And here we are, Cozmo AI now has a US office.",
        "From that point, everything started falling into place."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Looking backward to summarize",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_close_imperative",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "Ends with a direct command \u2014 restrain, find, pour, don't wait \u2014 but one that comes from principle, not persuasion.",
      "examples": [
        "Restrain the urge to hire.",
        "Pour GTM fuel on the fire, and scale dollars efficiently.",
        "You don't have to wait for a crisis to make time for your people."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Passive conclusions or observations",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_close_quiet_confidence",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "closing",
      "description": "Ends with an understatement that implies enormous confidence without stating it. The most power is in what's not said.",
      "examples": [
        "That's when we realized we were onto something.",
        "The mentor became the partner.",
        "My mentor has become our partner."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Overexplaining the significance",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_not_x_but_y",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Structures arguments as contrast: Not X \u2014 Y. Creates clean intellectual slot. Positions founder as someone who sees through conventional framing.",
      "examples": [
        "Not just agents \u2014 employees.",
        "Not cost optimization \u2014 leverage.",
        "Not market maturity \u2014 market penetration.",
        "Not demos \u2014 outcomes."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Linear arguments without contrast pivot",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_story_then_lesson",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Leads with the specific story or moment, then extracts the lesson. Never leads with the lesson and backfills a story.",
      "examples": [
        "Walking into Wells Fargo \u2192 insight about market penetration",
        "Hiring AEs at $1M \u2192 lesson about process maturity",
        "Car accident \u2192 realization about family priorities"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Leading with Here are 3 lessons I learned",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_thesis_evidence_principle",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Three-part structure: state the bold thesis, provide specific evidence (numbers, names, scenes), land on a transferable principle.",
      "examples": [
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a vanity metric. \u2192 Here's what we learned. \u2192 Restrain the urge to hire.",
        "AI is leverage not cost. \u2192 Telco +14%, logistics 3x. \u2192 Cost optimization is a finite game."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Rambling structure without clear arc",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_numbered_list_sparingly",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Uses numbered lists only for genuinely enumerable items \u2014 hiring criteria, product capabilities, stages. Never for observations or lessons.",
      "examples": [
        "1. Committed is not live. 2. Customer success > Sales.",
        "1. Real-time reasoning. 2. Voice infrastructure. 3. Evals."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Numbered lists for insights that flow naturally as prose",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_single_paragraph_bomb",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Uses a single short paragraph \u2014 sometimes one sentence \u2014 as a deliberate structural shock between longer paragraphs.",
      "examples": [
        "That's when we realized we were onto something.",
        "The dogfight comes later.",
        "My mentor has become our partner."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Consistent paragraph length throughout",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_pivot_word",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Uses a single pivot word \u2014 But, And, So, Still, Now \u2014 at the start of a sentence or paragraph to create a structural hinge.",
      "examples": [
        "But that 'next week' kept slipping.",
        "And here we are, Cozmo AI now has a US office.",
        "Still, we cared deeply about customer experience."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Long transitional phrases",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_customer_case_structure",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Structures customer case studies as: setup \u2192 unexpected result \u2192 dependency revelation. The reversal is the story.",
      "examples": [
        "Customer used Liza to cut headcount. \u2192 Ended up hiring 4 people. \u2192 Panicked when Liza went down.",
        "Telco routed upsells to AI. \u2192 ARPU rose 14% in 6 weeks."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Feature-led customer case studies",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_analogy_reframe",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Uses a sharp analogy to reframe an argument in a single sentence, making it impossible to un-see.",
      "examples": [
        "That's like asking if a motorcycle 'replaces' a bicycle.",
        "When infrastructure companies stop picking winners, they become the house that always wins.",
        "That 19-year-old kid is the reason a million agents have Sanas running on their desktops today."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Extended analogies",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_ironic_admission",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Admits to a mistake, contradiction, or irony in the structure. The admission is the structural move that creates trust.",
      "examples": [
        "The irony isn't lost on me. Four years ago I convinced Nuha to leave the US.",
        "My biggest hiring mistake was hiring salespeople at $1M ARR.",
        "We kind of are stalling them."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Defensive hedging",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_implied_stakes",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Doesn't state the stakes explicitly \u2014 implies them through the weight of the language. Reader feels the stakes without being told.",
      "examples": [
        "This is where most startups fall flat.",
        "That's what makes you part of the top 5%.",
        "Once they say go, it's scale, scale, scale."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Explicitly stating why this matters",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_definition_inversion",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Defines something by what it is NOT before defining what it IS. The negation creates the space for the real definition.",
      "examples": [
        "AI agents aren't employees. Employees operate with context, memory, accountability.",
        "Voice AI isn't chatbots with speech wrappers.",
        "Synthetic data isn't an anchor. It's a booster."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Defining by positive attributes only",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_two_act_structure",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Post has two clear acts: the setup/problem (first half) and the resolution/insight (second half). The break is structural, not announced.",
      "examples": [
        "Tried distributed team. Was busy without moving faster. [break] Consolidated. Speed unlocked.",
        "Hired AEs at $1M ARR. They struggled. [break] Took sales back. 4-5 meetings a day. It's paying off."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Three or four act structures",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_scene_to_principle",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Starts with a physical scene (location, time, action) and ends with an abstract principle extracted from it.",
      "examples": [
        "Walked into Wells Fargo. [scene] The entire opportunity sits in the gap between understanding and deployment. [principle]",
        "Car accident. Parents' voices. [scene] No metric matters more than your people. [principle]"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Starting with the principle",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_confession_lesson",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Starts with a specific personal confession or mistake, ends with the transferable lesson learned.",
      "examples": [
        "My biggest hiring mistake was hiring salespeople at $1M ARR. [...] Restrain the urge to hire.",
        "I've barely spoken to my parents since I started building. [...] You don't have to wait for a crisis."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Generic lessons without personal grounding",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_struct_cumulative_evidence",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "structure",
      "description": "Builds evidence cumulatively \u2014 each sentence adds another proof point to the same thesis, creating overwhelming weight.",
      "examples": [
        "A telco saw ARPU rise 14%. A logistics player boosted dues recovery 3x. A B2C customer ended up hiring 4 people."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Single proof point conclusions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_short_long_variation",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Varies dramatically between ultra-short sentences (3-5 words) and longer ones (15-25 words). The variation creates reading momentum.",
      "examples": [
        "The dogfight comes later. / That gap between 'we understand this' and 'we've deployed this' is where the entire opportunity sits.",
        "The dogfight everyone warned us about is not here yet."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Uniform sentence length",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_staccato_burst",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses 3-5 rapid-fire short sentences in a row to create a burst of energy at a key moment.",
      "examples": [
        "Showed up at GITEX. Shared what we were building. Inbound interest exploded.",
        "Committed is not live. Happy, but not growing. Shut a few down."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Long paragraphs during high-energy moments",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_long_wind_up",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses a longer, slower wind-up paragraph before delivering the key insight in a short, punchy sentence.",
      "examples": [
        "After attending 7 events in 12 days and watching startup after startup fall flat by indexing on demos... [long setup] We stopped demoing. [punch]"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Short setup before long payoff",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_em_dash_pause",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses em-dash to create a dramatic pause before a key phrase, creating rhythmic emphasis.",
      "examples": [
        "They became the house that always wins.",
        "That's when we realized \u2014 we were onto something.",
        "Because it delays reality."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Comma pauses for emphasis",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_repetition",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Repeats a word or phrase deliberately for rhythmic effect and emphasis. The repetition signals importance.",
      "examples": [
        "scale, scale, scale",
        "build, pivot, learn, scale",
        "show that you can survive the production traffic"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Varying language to avoid repetition",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_fragment_power",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses grammatically incomplete fragments for specific rhetorical effect. The incompleteness creates punch.",
      "examples": [
        "Exciting days ahead.",
        "The dogfight comes later.",
        "Because it delays reality."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Complete sentences for all rhetorical punches",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_question_and_answer",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Poses a question within the post and immediately answers it in the next sentence, creating a rapid-fire Q&A rhythm.",
      "examples": [
        "What's common in all these companies? They're all gateways into synthetic data.",
        "Why does this matter? Because customers don't care about your training tricks."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Unanswered questions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_colon_reveal",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses a colon to create a reveal moment \u2014 sets up anticipation, then delivers the specific.",
      "examples": [
        "That's when we realized: we were onto something.",
        "The solution is simple: seed with real data, boost with synthetic."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Telling the reveal before the setup",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_parenthetical",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses parentheticals for quick asides \u2014 humor, qualification, or emphasis \u2014 without breaking the main rhythm.",
      "examples": [
        "We pivoted from Zywa to CozmoX AI (YC W22).",
        "Khaleeji and Masri Arabic (not just standard Arabic).",
        "The voice AI world is not there yet. (Not even close.)"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Long parenthetical explanations",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rhythm_line_breaks",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhythm",
      "description": "Uses strategic line breaks between paragraphs to create breathing room at key structural moments. Short paragraphs can be single sentences.",
      "examples": [
        "Full circle moment for me. [line break] Four years ago, I left Strategic Gears to start Zywa."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Long unbroken paragraphs",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_earned_vulnerability",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Vulnerability is specific and earned, never performed. Admits mistakes with precise detail. Doesn't wallow \u2014 lands on a lesson.",
      "examples": [
        "We fired 4 customers last week, 5% of our revenue.",
        "My biggest hiring mistake was hiring salespeople at $1M ARR.",
        "I've barely spoken to my parents since I started building."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Generic vulnerability without specific details",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_patient_confidence",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "States controversial or counterintuitive positions with quiet certainty, no defensiveness, no over-explanation.",
      "examples": [
        "The real use is building leverage.",
        "There's no real dogfight in voice AI.",
        "The TAM for AI Agents isn't limited by headcount."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Over-justifying controversial positions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_irony_self_awareness",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Uses self-aware irony to disarm reader. Acknowledges contradictions without performing humility.",
      "examples": [
        "The irony isn't lost on me. Four years ago, I convinced Nuha to leave the US.",
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric. On paper, it looks great."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Taking oneself too seriously",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_direct_naming",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Names specific companies, people, and products rather than speaking vaguely. The specificity is the credibility.",
      "examples": [
        "Wells Fargo. Strategic Gears. PeakXV. AWS. TechCrunch Disrupt. Nuha Hashem."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Vague references to industry, competitors, or people",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_builder_intensity",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Communicates the intensity and urgency of building \u2014 days counted, meetings counted, interviews counted. Numbers signal intensity.",
      "examples": [
        "4-5 meetings a day.",
        "7 events in 12 days.",
        "90 interviews in three weeks.",
        "7 engineers in 7 days."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Casual or understated intensity",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_genuine_excitement",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Expresses genuine excitement without hype words. The excitement is specific, earned, and grounded in evidence.",
      "examples": [
        "Exciting days ahead.",
        "Super excited for what's ahead.",
        "This is why I keep saying..."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Generic excitement without evidence",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_no_hedging",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Makes direct statements without hedge words \u2014 maybe, perhaps, I think, arguably. The conviction is clear.",
      "examples": [
        "The real use is building leverage.",
        "There's no real dogfight in voice AI.",
        "You can't pay your way into PMF."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Hedge-heavy language that softens conviction",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_technical_credibility",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Deploys technical terms precisely when they add credibility \u2014 APE, ASR/TTS, BFSI, FDT \u2014 but doesn't over-explain.",
      "examples": [
        "Agent Protocol Engine (APE).",
        "Low-latency ASR/TTS.",
        "Real-time reasoning AI Employees for BFSI."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Technical jargon for jargon's sake",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_defiant_clarity",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "States positions that challenge mainstream consensus without being combative. The clarity is the defiance.",
      "examples": [
        "Disruption is never quiet, it's never comfortable and it has to be spoken out LOUD!",
        "We're here to replace human cost, not human talent.",
        "OpenAI just dropped the biggest bomb. This is the mainstream narrative. It sells."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Aggressive or confrontational tone",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_warm_authority",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Combines genuine warmth (team mentions, co-founder credit, gratitude) with authority (bold positions, hard decisions). The warmth makes the authority palatable.",
      "examples": [
        "My mentor has become our partner.",
        "Nuha and I spent three intense weeks.",
        "Talk with Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Cold authority without warmth",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_intellectual_delight",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Expresses genuine intellectual pleasure when encountering a sharp question or counterintuitive insight.",
      "examples": [
        "I really love such questions because they reveal an outdated model.",
        "Who knew FBI hostage negotiation tactics could boost debt collections?",
        "What really struck me wasn't the product announcements."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Dismissing or deflecting sharp questions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_founder_to_founder",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Speaks directly to founders \u2014 as a peer, not an authority. Shares specifically what worked and what didn't without moralizing.",
      "examples": [
        "Restrain the urge to hire until you have a repeatable playbook.",
        "If you've been grinding nonstop, maybe this is your cue to pause and call home."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Lecturing tone from a position of authority",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_quiet_confidence_in_process",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Expresses confidence in the process and the mission, even during uncertainty. The confidence is in the direction, not the outcome.",
      "examples": [
        "Our mission stays the same.",
        "We still have our team in the UAE.",
        "Things have truly come full circle."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Overconfidence or hype about outcomes",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_honest_about_what_failed",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "Names what didn't work clearly and specifically, without excessive hedging or reframing as a 'learning.'",
      "examples": [
        "People have zero ownership.",
        "We were busy without moving faster.",
        "Some customers promised scale but never activated meaningfully."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Euphemistic language for failure",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_tone_team_as_collective",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "tone",
      "description": "References the team as a collective \u2014 we, our team, Nuha and I, Talal/Issam/Wesley \u2014 never as subordinates.",
      "examples": [
        "Nuha and I spent three intense weeks.",
        "My team has been quietly keeping things moving.",
        "Talk with Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "I-centric language that erases the team",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_we_for_company",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "Uses 'we' for company decisions and strategic actions \u2014 the company acts as a unit.",
      "examples": [
        "We stopped demoing.",
        "We fired 4 customers.",
        "We've been driving our US GTM motion ourselves.",
        "We just sunset our entire Account Management team."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Using 'I' for company-level decisions",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_i_for_personal",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "Uses 'I' for personal reflections, mistakes admitted, lessons learned, observations from field.",
      "examples": [
        "I realized.",
        "I walked into a Wells Fargo branch.",
        "My biggest hiring mistake.",
        "I've barely spoken to my parents."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Using 'we' for personal reflections",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_you_direct_address",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "Uses 'you' to address fellow founders directly \u2014 providing actionable advice or asking them to consider something.",
      "examples": [
        "Restrain the urge to hire.",
        "You don't have to wait for a crisis.",
        "If you want to do something, just freaking do it."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Third-person generalization when speaking to founders",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_they_for_enterprise",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "Uses 'they' to describe enterprise buyers' behavior, decisions, and reactions \u2014 humanizing the enterprise.",
      "examples": [
        "They uploaded them to our AI employee.",
        "They ended up hiring 4 new people.",
        "They said: We don't care if you compete with our products."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Abstract references to 'the enterprise' or 'customers'",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_nuha_i_duo",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "References Nuha and Alok as a duo \u2014 'Nuha and I' \u2014 establishing the co-founder partnership as the unit of action.",
      "examples": [
        "Nuha and I spent three intense weeks.",
        "Alok and I have switched gears.",
        "Four years ago, I convinced Nuha to leave the US."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Singular founder narrative when co-founder was involved",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_pronoun_passive_avoidance",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "pronoun",
      "description": "Avoids passive voice. Actions have agents. Someone did something. The actor is always named.",
      "examples": [
        "We stopped demoing.",
        "I walked into Wells Fargo.",
        "The AI Employee assigned calls to seven reps."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Passive constructions like 'it was realized' or 'it was decided'",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_vocab_no_jargon",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "vocabulary",
      "description": "Avoids AI jargon that has lost meaning \u2014 leverage (as verb), navigate, landscape, foster, harness, unlock, elevate, empower, game-changer.",
      "examples": [
        "The real use is building leverage.",
        "We're replacing that with AI Employees."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "leverage as verb, navigate, landscape, game-changer, paradigm shift",

    {
      "id": "style_vocab_numbers_as_anchors",

    {
      "id": "style_format_short_paragraphs",

      "id": "style_format_bullet_points_sparingly",

      "examples": [

      "anti_pattern": "Bullet points for insights or lessons",

    {
      "id": "style_format_bold_for_names",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",
      "description": "Occasionally bolds company names, product names, or key numbers when they serve as anchors \u2014 not for emphasis of opinions.",
      "examples": [
        "Cozmo AI (YC W22)",
        "$1M ARR"

      "anti_pattern": "Bolding for rhetorical emphasis",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_format_sentence_case",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",

      "examples": [

    {
      "id": "style_format_line_break_as_breath",
      "node_type": "style_rule",

    {
      "id": "style_format_colon_list_integration",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",
      "description": "Integrates lists into prose using a colon rather than creating separate bullet point sections.",

    {
      "id": "style_format_linkedin_length",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",
      "description": "LinkedIn posts run 200-400 words for opinion posts, 100-200 words for announcements. Never blog-post length.",
      "examples": [

      "anti_pattern": "Posts over 500 words",
      "platform": "linkedin"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_format_no_headers",
      "node_type": "style_rule",

      "examples": [

      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Bold headers in LinkedIn posts",
      "platform": "linkedin"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_format_numbers_digits",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",
      "description": "Uses numerals for numbers in LinkedIn posts \u2014 4, 7, 12, 14%, 90+ \u2014 not spelled out, even at the start of sentences.",
      "examples": [
        "7 events in 12 days.",
        "4-5 meetings a day.",
        "14% ARPU increase."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Spelling out numbers like 'seven events in twelve days'",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_format_tag_team_members",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "formatting",
      "description": "Tags specific team members by name \u2014 Nuha, Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna \u2014 especially in posts about company events or achievements.",
      "examples": [
        "Talk with Talal, Issam, Wesley, Sayan, Krishna.",
        "Nuha and I."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Anonymous team references",
      "platform": "linkedin"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_contrast_pair",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Deploys a contrast pair as the structural spine of the post. The entire post is an argument between two opposing forces.",
      "examples": [
        "Leverage vs cost optimization.",
        "Market maturity vs market penetration.",
        "Demo culture vs outcomes culture."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Single-track arguments without tension",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_counter_narrative",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Opens with what everyone else believes, then systematically dismantles it with evidence.",
      "examples": [
        "Everyone is underestimating the AI Agent market.",
        "Everyone told us the Bay Area would be a bloodbath. It's not.",
        "OpenAI just dropped the biggest bomb. This is the mainstream narrative."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Presenting only one's own view",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_specific_before_general",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Provides the specific example before stating the general principle. The specific earns the right to generalize.",
      "examples": [
        "A telco saw ARPU rise 14% in six weeks. [specific] \u2192 The real use is building leverage. [general]"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Leading with the general principle",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_reversal",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Sets up an expected outcome, then reveals the opposite. The reversal is the insight.",
      "examples": [
        "Customer planned to cut headcount. Ended up hiring 4 people.",
        "The irony isn't lost on me. Four years ago I convinced Nuha to leave the US."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Expected linear outcomes",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_second_order",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Pushes beyond the obvious first-order effect to name the second-order or third-order consequence.",
      "examples": [
        "AI doesn't just optimize what humans do \u2014 it reveals what humans can't see.",
        "Cost optimization is a finite game. But leverage creation is infinite."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "First-order observations only",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_earned_cynicism",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Expresses skepticism about market narratives that have been personally tested and found wanting. Not cynicism as a pose \u2014 cynicism earned through experience.",
      "examples": [
        "Speed to $1M ARR is a noisy vanity metric.",
        "The dogfight everyone warned us about is not here yet."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Unearned cynicism or pessimism",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_scale_to_personal",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Moves from large-scale insight (AI market, enterprise adoption) to deeply personal story (car accident, parents) in the same structural vocabulary.",

      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Keeping scale and personal entirely separate",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_anticipate_objection",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Names the obvious objection to the position, then addresses it directly within the post.",
      "examples": [
        "I can just hire someone for $5 an hour to make cold calls. Why pay for an AI Employee? [objection] Because they reveal an outdated model. [response]"
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Ignoring obvious objections",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_metaphor_snap",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Uses a single crisp metaphor that captures the entire argument in one image or phrase.",
      "examples": [
        "The house that always wins.",
        "Duct-taped together.",
        "Zombie contacts.",
        "White space."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Extended metaphors over multiple paragraphs",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_forcing_function",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Names the external commitment or forcing function that forced a decision \u2014 sponsoring a conference, setting a deadline.",
      "examples": [
        "We sponsored TechCrunch Disrupt. That became our line in the sand."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Decisions that appear to happen naturally",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_numbers_in_threes",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Groups specific examples or evidence in threes for rhythmic completeness.",
      "examples": [
        "A telco saw ARPU rise 14%. A logistics player boosted recovery 3x. A B2C customer hired 4 new people.",
        "build, pivot, learn, scale",
        "Scale, scale, scale."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Long lists of examples",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_implied_stakes",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Implies rather than states the stakes \u2014 the reader feels the weight without being told how to feel.",

        "Once they say go, it's scale, scale, scale."

      "anti_pattern": "Explicitly stating why this matters",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_geographic_proof",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Uses specific geographic locations as proof points \u2014 not 'a bank' but 'a Wells Fargo branch', not 'a customer in the Gulf' but 'Riyadh to Nairobi'.",
      "examples": [

        "A customer win in Riyadh helped our AI employee close a deal in Nairobi."

      "anti_pattern": "Generic geographic references",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_present_tense_for_past",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Uses present tense to narrate past events, creating immediacy and proximity.",

      "anti_pattern": "Past tense for past events",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "style_rheto_understatement",
      "node_type": "style_rule",
      "rule_type": "rhetorical_move",
      "description": "Deliberately understates a significant achievement or moment, letting the reader feel its significance.",
      "examples": [
        "My mentor has become our partner.",
        "That's when we realized we were onto something.",
        "Exciting days ahead."
      ],
      "anti_pattern": "Over-explaining significance",
      "platform": "universal"
    },
    {
      "id": "model_leverage_not_cost",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Leverage > Cost Optimization",
      "description": "Evaluates every AI deployment through leverage creation (infinite, compounding) vs cost cutting (finite, capped at 90%). When a customer says reduce headcount, reframe toward revenue expansion, market creation, and learning propagation. The question is not how much can you cut \u2014 it's how much can you multiply.",
      "priority": 10
    },
    {
      "id": "model_demo_outcome_ladder",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Demo to Outcome Maturity Ladder",
      "description": "Classifies enterprise AI readiness: Level 1 (impressed by demos), Level 2 (asking about production traffic), Level 3 (demanding outcome guarantees), Level 4 (integrating AI employees into core workflows). Cozmo targets Level 3-4. Stop demoing, start understanding.",
      "priority": 9
    },
    {
      "id": "model_maturity_penetration",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Market Maturity not Market Penetration",
      "description": "Distinguishes markets where people understand technology vs where it's deployed. Bay Area understands voice AI but hasn't deployed it. The gap between understanding and deployment is where the entire opportunity sits. Applied to evaluate any enterprise AI market.",
      "priority": 9
    },
    {
      "id": "model_sales_timing",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Founder-Led Sales Timing Model",
      "description": "When to hand off sales: $1M ARR too early (motion isn't repeatable), $3-5M ARR is the sweet spot (process stabilized). Before handoff, founders must run 4-5 meetings/day to build the playbook that eventually transfers to AEs.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_forcing_function",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "External Forcing Function",
      "description": "When a strategic decision keeps getting delayed, create an external commitment that makes inaction impossible. Sponsor a conference. Sign a lease. Make a public announcement. Applied to the US move via TechCrunch Disrupt sponsorship.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_infra_neutrality",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Neutrality as Infrastructure Moat",
      "description": "Infrastructure companies that stop picking winners and simply enable the game become the house that always wins. Applied to evaluate platform partnerships. Derived from AWS re:Invent insight. Cozmo applies this to its own infrastructure positioning.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "model_dogfooding_loop",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Dogfooding to PMF Loop",
      "description": "Use your own product for revenue-critical workflows. Bugs and edge cases found through dogfooding are impossible to discover through QA alone. If you won't trust your product to sell itself, why should a customer? Applied: 90% of Cozmo meetings booked by own AI employees.",
      "priority": 9
    },
    {
      "id": "model_busy_vs_fast",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Busy vs Actually Moving Fast",
      "description": "Distributed teams create coordination overhead that masquerades as productivity. The team is busy \u2014 meetings, standups, async updates \u2014 but shipping velocity drops. Fix: collocate until the playbook exists, then distribute.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_s_curve_stacking",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Multiple S-Curve Career Model",
      "description": "Best talent stacks multiple S-curves, each compounding on the last. A pivot from Zywa to CozmoX wasn't a restart \u2014 it was the next S-curve standing on top of infrastructure, customer, and speed learnings from the previous one.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "model_passion_hiring",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Hire Passion Not Availability",
      "description": "People looking for a job optimize for stability. Passionate people who already have jobs optimize for impact. The first fills seats; the second builds companies. Applied to every hiring decision at Cozmo.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_consumer_enterprise_gtm",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Consumer GTM vs Enterprise GTM",
      "description": "Consumer: campaigns, promos \u2014 you can buy your way in. Enterprise: trust earned through deployment, proof over promises. You can't performance-market your way into a procurement committee. Different playbooks entirely.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "model_context_propagation",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Context Propagation Model",
      "description": "A learning from one customer or region propagates to all others through the AI system. The system that learns from Riyadh closes deals in Nairobi an hour later. Context compounds; headcount doesn't.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_production_traffic_test",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Production Traffic as True Product Test",
      "description": "The only real product test is production traffic. Demos prove demo quality. Production proves product quality. 10,000+ calls/sec, real user behavior, real edge cases. Lab conditions are fiction.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "model_fdt_over_cs",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Forward Deployed Team vs Customer Success",
      "description": "Traditional CS manages relationships. FDT owns outcomes. Engineers deployed at customer sites ensuring implementation actually works. The difference between hoping customers succeed and ensuring they do.",
      "priority": 8
    },
    {
      "id": "model_committed_not_live",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Committed ARR vs Live ARR",
      "description": "Committed ARR looks good on paper. Live ARR tells the truth. The gap between them reveals ICP alignment, customer velocity, and operational readiness. Never confuse committed with live.",
      "priority": 9
    },
    {
      "id": "model_real_data_anchor",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Real Data Anchor with Synthetic Boost",
      "description": "Build AI models on a foundation of real data, however small. Boost with synthetic. Keep pulling back to reality when models drift. The anchor is always reality. Applied to all AI training decisions.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "model_second_time_founder",
      "node_type": "thinking_model",
      "name": "Second-Time Founder Playbook",
      "description": "First-time founders plan, research, and theorize. Second-time founders start selling. No endless slide decks. No bloated market research. Just start. Applied to the Zywa-to-Cozmo pivot.",
      "priority": 8

    {
      "id": "sb_leverage_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Leverage reveals invisible opportunities",
      "description": "Liza uncovered hundreds of opportunities the human team had completely missed. The leverage isn't in doing the same work faster \u2014 it's in seeing what humans can't see at all.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_leverage_not_cost"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_leverage_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Leverage compounds across regions in minutes",
      "description": "A win in Riyadh propagates to Nairobi in an hour. Human learning takes off-sites and hundreds of thousands of dollars. AI learning is instantaneous.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_leverage_not_cost"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_leverage_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Cost optimization is a finite ceiling not a floor",
      "description": "You can cut costs by 90% and hit a hard ceiling. There is no 90% limit on revenue expansion, new market creation, or learning propagation.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_leverage_not_cost"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_leverage_04",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "60 percent of customers came to out-sell not cut costs",
      "description": "The data is clear: most Cozmo customers came to expand, not shrink. The market is signaling what AI should be used for.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_leverage_not_cost"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_employee_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Agents are territorial employees are orchestrated",
      "description": "An agent owns one function. An employee coordinates across functions \u2014 reading intent, routing work, managing other agents and humans.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_ai_employees_not_agents"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_employee_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Memory is what separates employees from agents",
      "description": "An agent starts fresh every conversation. An employee remembers the customer from last week, the objection from last month, the deal from last year.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_ai_employees_not_agents"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_employee_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Accountability changes the product entirely",
      "description": "When the AI employee is accountable for outcomes \u2014 not just task completion \u2014 the architecture, pricing, and customer relationship changes fundamentally.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_ai_employees_not_agents"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_employee_04",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "APE is the infrastructure that makes employees possible",
      "description": "Agent Protocol Engine powers real-time reasoning at 10,000+ calls/sec. Without the infrastructure layer, you have agents, not employees.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_ai_employees_not_agents"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_cs_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Models change every quarter the implementation doesnt",
      "description": "GPT-4 was replaced by GPT-4o. The underlying model is always changing. The deployment, the guardrails, the integration \u2014 those are sticky.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_cs_only_moat"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_cs_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "FDT engineers own outcomes not just relationships",
      "description": "Traditional CS asks how are things going. FDT engineers ask what needs to work and then make it work. The difference is accountability.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_cs_only_moat"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_cs_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Buyers put their careers on the line for you",
      "description": "The internal champion who chose Cozmo AI is betting their next performance review on your product working. That's the trust you're responsible for.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_cs_only_moat"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_sales_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "At 1M ARR wins look like patterns but arent repeatable",
      "description": "Three wins in three months look like a motion. They're not. They're founder-dependent, context-dependent, timing-dependent. The pattern is an illusion.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_no_sales_before_3m"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_sales_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "AI pitches must pivot mid-call in real time",
      "description": "A product that evolves weekly means objections you haven't encountered before every call. Only founders have the context to handle this in real time.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_no_sales_before_3m"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_sales_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "3 to 5M ARR is when the motion stabilizes enough to transfer",
      "description": "By $3-5M ARR, you've seen enough sales cycles to document the playbook. You know the ICP, the objections, the pricing, the proof points.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_no_sales_before_3m"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_market_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Wells Fargo had zero AI deployed in branches",
      "description": "Walked into a Wells Fargo branch in the Valley. No voice AI, no automation. The most sophisticated market in the world is still duct-taped together.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_maturity_not_penetration"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_market_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Failed pilots created cautious but educated buyers",
      "description": "Enterprises burned by over-promised AI are now the best prospects. They know what bad looks like and won't waste time on demos.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_maturity_not_penetration"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_market_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The dogfight comes when delivery becomes predictable",
      "description": "When any company can reliably deploy voice AI, price drops and distribution becomes the only moat. We're nowhere near that yet.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_maturity_not_penetration"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_demo_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Latency and human-like voice are table stakes now",
      "description": "These were impressive 18 months ago. Enterprise now treats them as baseline requirements, not differentiators. The bar has moved.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_demos_dead"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_demo_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Top 5 percent is understanding enterprise reality not showing product",
      "description": "The companies that win deployments spend more time understanding the enterprise's workflows, pain points, and tech stack than demoing their product.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_demos_dead"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_demo_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Production traffic survival is the real product test",
      "description": "10,000+ calls/sec. Real users. Real edge cases. Real failures and recoveries. Nothing before production traffic counts as proof.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_demos_dead"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_dog_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "90 percent of Cozmo meetings booked by AI employees",
      "description": "If you won't trust your own product with your own revenue-critical workflows, why should a customer? This is the simplest proof of conviction.",

    },
    {
      "id": "sb_dog_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Dogfooding reveals edge cases QA never could",
      "description": "Real usage in real conditions finds failure modes that no test suite captures. This is why the first version shipped in weeks, not months.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_dogfooding"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_dog_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The product that sells itself gets the product-market fit signal fastest",
      "description": "When AI employees are booking your own meetings, every interaction is a data point on what's working and what's breaking.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_dogfooding"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_hire_01",

      "topic": "Every early hire is either an accelerator or a silent eroder",
      "description": "There is no neutral. A hire who brings the right energy multiplies the culture. A hire who treats it as a job silently erodes it.",

      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The first 50 roles set the template for the next 500",

      "id": "sb_hire_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",

      "parent_belief": "belief_hiring_dna"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_voice_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Milliseconds matter in a way they never do in text",
      "description": "A 200ms delay in a text chatbot is imperceptible. A 200ms delay in voice is an awkward pause. Voice has real-time constraints that change everything.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_voice_ai_playbook"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_voice_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Interrupt handling requires a completely different architecture",
      "description": "When someone starts talking while the AI is still speaking, what happens? Text doesn't have this problem. Voice requires a dedicated architectural layer.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_voice_ai_playbook"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_voice_03",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Context must survive over multiple turns in real time",
      "description": "In text, context sits in the conversation history. In voice, the model must track context while simultaneously processing and generating speech.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_voice_ai_playbook"
    },

      "id": "sb_ctx_01",

      "topic": "Headcount scales linearly context compounds exponentially",

      "parent_belief": "belief_context_compounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_reg_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "500 plus guardrails per industry are not replicable quickly",
      "description": "Building industry-specific guardrails for BFSI requires deep regulatory knowledge accumulated over deployments. This is not a feature \u2014 it's years of accumulated compliance expertise.",

      "id": "sb_reg_02",

      "topic": "Enterprise compliance requirements create natural switching costs",

      "id": "sb_infra_01",

      "description": "Salesforce and Microsoft guard territory, but AI is moving too fast for territory guarding to work. AWS bet on the game, not the players.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_infra_neutrality"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_people_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Founders who never call home are building on a hollow foundation",

    },
    {
      "id": "sb_people_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The people who ask Did you eat are the most important stakeholders",

      "parent_belief": "belief_people_over_metrics"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_2nd_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Second-time founders ship before they're ready and learn from reality",
      "description": "First-time founders wait until the product is perfect. Second-time founders know the product is never perfect. Reality is the only real teacher.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_second_time_no_bs"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_2nd_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The Zywa learnings applied to CozmoX from day one",
      "description": "Every pattern learned building Zywa \u2014 customer relationships, hiring standards, GTM approach \u2014 compressed the CozmoX trajectory. The second S-curve starts higher.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_second_time_no_bs"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_arr_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Committed is not live and live is not growing",
      "description": "Three states: committed (signed), live (deployed), growing (expanding). Most ARR charts count committed. The only metric that matters is growing.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_arr_vanity"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_arr_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "A 1M ARR sprint that leaves misaligned customers is net negative",
      "description": "Five customers who don't scale drain more engineering time and customer success attention than two customers who do. Revenue isn't the score. Scalable revenue is.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_arr_vanity"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_pmf_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "Enterprise buyers don't respond to performance marketing",
      "description": "No enterprise champion ever said 'I saw your LinkedIn ad and decided to run a procurement process.' Enterprise trust is built through reference customers, deployments, and founder conversations.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_cant_buy_pmf"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_pmf_02",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "The proof is always in the production deployment",
      "description": "Pilots prove pilot quality. References prove reference quality. Only sustained production deployment proves product quality.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_cant_buy_pmf"
    },
    {
      "id": "sb_tam_01",
      "node_type": "sub_belief",
      "topic": "One agent per human is the wrong unit of analysis",
      "description": "The question isn't how many agents to replace how many humans. The question is what workflows become possible when there's no limit on simultaneous processes.",
      "parent_belief": "belief_tam_imagination"
